([Norwegian](/wiki/Norwegian_language "Norwegian language"): *De 17. olympiske vinterleker*; [Nynorsk](/wiki/Nynorsk_language "Nynorsk language"): *Dei 17. olympiske vinterleikane*) and commonly known as **Lillehammer '94**, were an international winter [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") held from 12 to 27 February 1994 in and around [Lillehammer](/wiki/Lillehammer "Lillehammer"), Norway. Having lost the bid for the [1992 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1992_Winter_Olympics "1992 Winter Olympics") to [Albertville](/wiki/Albertville "Albertville") in France, Lillehammer was awarded the 1994 Winter Games on 15 September 1988, two days before the [1988 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1988_Summer_Olympics "1988 Summer Olympics") [opening ceremonies](/wiki/1988_Summer_Olympics_opening_ceremony "1988 Summer Olympics opening ceremony") at the 94th [IOC Session](/wiki/IOC_Session "IOC Session") in [Seoul](/wiki/Seoul "Seoul"), South Korea. Due to the calendar changes made in 1986, this was the only time that the [Winter Olympics](/wiki/Winter_Olympic_Games "Winter Olympic Games") took place two years after the previous Winter Games, and the first to be held in a different year from the [Summer Olympics](/wiki/Summer_Olympic_Games "Summer Olympic Games"). This was the first Winter Olympics that took place in a year with the [Asian Games](/wiki/Asian_Games "Asian Games"), [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games"), and [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup"). This was the second Olympic Games of any type hosted in Norway — the first being the [1952 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1952_Winter_Olympics "1952 Winter Olympics") in [Oslo](/wiki/Oslo "Oslo") — and the fourth Olympics overall to be held in a [Nordic country](/wiki/Nordic_countries "Nordic countries"), after the [1912 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1912_Summer_Olympics "1912 Summer Olympics") in [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm"), Sweden, and the [1952 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1952_Summer_Olympics "1952 Summer Olympics") in [Helsinki](/wiki/Helsinki "Helsinki"), Finland. Lillehammer is the northernmost city ever to host the [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games").

The **1994 Winter Olympics**, officially known as the **XVII Olympic Winter Games** ([Norwegian](/wiki/Norwegian_language "Norwegian language"): *De 17. olympiske vinterleker*; [Nynorsk](/wiki/Nynorsk_language "Nynorsk language"): *Dei 17. olympiske vinterleikane*) and commonly known as **Lillehammer '94**, were an international winter [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") held from 12 to 27 February 1994 in and around [Lillehammer](/wiki/Lillehammer "Lillehammer"), Norway. Having lost the bid for the [1992 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1992_Winter_Olympics "1992 Winter Olympics") to [Albertville](/wiki/Albertville "Albertville") in France, Lillehammer was awarded the 1994 Winter Games on 15 September 1988, two days before the [1988 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1988_Summer_Olympics "1988 Summer Olympics") [opening ceremonies](/wiki/1988_Summer_Olympics_opening_ceremony "1988 Summer Olympics opening ceremony") at the 94th [IOC Session](/wiki/IOC_Session "IOC Session") in [Seoul](/wiki/Seoul "Seoul"), South Korea. Due to the calendar changes made in 1986, this was the only time that the [Winter Olympics](/wiki/Winter_Olympic_Games "Winter Olympic Games") took place two years after the previous Winter Games, and the first to be held in a different year from the [Summer Olympics](/wiki/Summer_Olympic_Games "Summer Olympic Games"). This was the first Winter Olympics that took place in a year with the [Asian Games](/wiki/Asian_Games "Asian Games"), [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games"), and [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup"). This was the second Olympic Games of any type hosted in Norway — the first being the [1952 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1952_Winter_Olympics "1952 Winter Olympics") in [Oslo](/wiki/Oslo "Oslo") — and the fourth Olympics overall to be held in a [Nordic country](/wiki/Nordic_countries "Nordic countries"), after the [1912 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1912_Summer_Olympics "1912 Summer Olympics") in [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm"), Sweden, and the [1952 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1952_Summer_Olympics "1952 Summer Olympics") in [Helsinki](/wiki/Helsinki "Helsinki"), Finland. Lillehammer is the northernmost city ever to host the [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games").

Although Lillehammer Municipality was the main host, some events were held in neighboring municipalities, and the speed skating events were held in [Hamar Municipality](/wiki/Hamar_Municipality "Hamar Municipality"), some [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 1994 Winter Olympics") matches were played in [Gjøvik Municipality](/wiki/Gj%C3%B8vik_Municipality "Gjøvik Municipality"), and the [Alpine skiing](/wiki/Alpine_skiing_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Alpine skiing at the 1994 Winter Olympics") events were held in [Øyer Municipality](/wiki/%C3%98yer_Municipality "Øyer Municipality") and [Ringebu Municipality](/wiki/Ringebu_Municipality "Ringebu Municipality"). Sixty-seven [National Olympic Committees](/wiki/National_Olympic_Committees "National Olympic Committees") and 1,737 athletes participated in six [sports](/wiki/Olympic_sports "Olympic sports") and sixty-one events.[[1]](#cite_note-athletes_number-2) Fourteen countries made their
Olympic debuts, of which nine were former [Soviet](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") republics. The Games also saw the introduction of stricter and more rigid qualifying rules, reducing the number of under-performing participants. Six new events were introduced into the Olympic programme: new distances in [short-track speed skating](/wiki/Short-track_speed_skating_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Short-track speed skating at the 1994 Winter Olympics") and [aerials](/wiki/Freestyle_skiing_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Freestyle skiing at the 1994 Winter Olympics"), and [speed skating](/wiki/Speed_skating_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Speed skating at the 1994 Winter Olympics") events were held indoors. Almost two million people spectated at the Games, which were the first to have the [Olympic Truce](/wiki/Olympic_Truce "Olympic Truce") in effect. The Olympics were followed by the [1994 Winter Paralympics](/wiki/1994_Winter_Paralympics "1994 Winter Paralympics") from 10 to 19 March.

[Manuela Di Centa](/wiki/Manuela_Di_Centa "Manuela Di Centa") and [Lyubov Yegorova](/wiki/Lyubov_Yegorova_(cross-country_skier) "Lyubov Yegorova (cross-country skier)") dominated women's [cross-country skiing](/wiki/Cross-country_skiing_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Cross-country skiing at the 1994 Winter Olympics"), taking five and four medals for [Italy](/wiki/Italy_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Italy at the 1994 Winter Olympics") and [Russia](/wiki/Russia_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Russia at the 1994 Winter Olympics") respectively. A crowd of more than 100,000 saw Italy beat [Norway](/wiki/Norway_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Norway at the 1994 Winter Olympics") by 0.4 seconds in the [men's 4 × 10 km relay](/wiki/Cross-country_skiing_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_4_%C3%97_10_kilometre_relay "Cross-country skiing at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Men's 4 × 10 kilometre relay"). [Vreni Schneider](/wiki/Vreni_Schneider "Vreni Schneider") won a complete set of medals for [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Switzerland at the 1994 Winter Olympics") in Alpine skiing, while Norway took a [podium sweep](/wiki/Podium_sweep "Podium sweep") in the [men's combined](/wiki/Alpine_skiing_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_combined "Alpine skiing at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Men's combined") competition. In [ladies' singles](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Ladies%27_singles "Figure skating at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Ladies' singles") figure skating, 16-year-old Ukrainian [Oksana Baiul](/wiki/Oksana_Baiul "Oksana Baiul") won gold, narrowly defeating [Nancy Kerrigan](/wiki/Nancy_Kerrigan "Nancy Kerrigan"), who won silver;[[2]](#cite_note-nyt940108-3) this was the first Ukrainian medal at the Olympics in their first independent appearance. [Johann Olav Koss](/wiki/Johann_Olav_Koss "Johann Olav Koss") won three speed skating golds for Norway, while 13-year-old [Kim Yun-mi](/wiki/Kim_Yun-mi_(speed_skater) "Kim Yun-mi (speed skater)") from South Korea became the youngest-ever Olympic gold medalist. [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Sweden at the 1994 Winter Olympics") defeated [Canada](/wiki/Canada_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 1994 Winter Olympics") in a dramatic [penalty shootout](/wiki/Penalty_shootout "Penalty shootout") in the [ice hockey final](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics#Gold_medal_game "Ice hockey at the 1994 Winter Olympics"). [Russia](/wiki/Russia_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Russia at the 1994 Winter Olympics") topped the medal table, winning 11 gold medals, while [Norway](/wiki/Norway_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Norway at the 1994 Winter Olympics") collected the highest number of medals overall, with 26. The event also gained notoriety for the [assault of Nancy Kerrigan](/wiki/Assault_of_Nancy_Kerrigan "Assault of Nancy Kerrigan").

## Host city selection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1994_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Host city selection")]

Main article: [Bids for the 1994 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Bids_for_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Bids for the 1994 Winter Olympics")

[![A map of Norway with Lillehammer in the middle towards the bottom](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Norway_location_map.svg/250px-Norway_location_map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Norway_location_map.svg "A map of Norway with Lillehammer in the middle towards the bottom")

![A map of Norway with Lillehammer in the middle towards the bottom](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Green_pog.svg/20px-Green_pog.svg.png)

Lillehammer

Location of Lillehammer in Norway

The idea for an Olympic bid for Norway was born in 1981, when [Falun](/wiki/Falun "Falun") in neighbouring [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") was defeated by [Calgary](/wiki/Calgary "Calgary") in [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") to host the [1988 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1988_Winter_Olympics "1988 Winter Olympics"). Along with the Norwegian government, the bid was also publicly encouraged and supported by the [Swedish government](/wiki/Government_of_Sweden "Government of Sweden"), largely to help stimulate the economy of their inland counties.[[3]](#cite_note-4) Lillehammer originally [bid for the 1992 Games](/wiki/Lillehammer_bid_for_the_1992_Winter_Olympics "Lillehammer bid for the 1992 Winter Olympics") but came fourth in the voting, with the Games ultimately awarded to [Albertville](/wiki/Albertville "Albertville"), France.[[4]](#cite_note-5) In 1986, at the 91st IOC Session in [Lausanne](/wiki/Lausanne "Lausanne"), the IOC announced the intent to separate the Summer and Winter editions (which had been held in the same year since the inception of the Winter Olympics in 1924) after the 1992 Games. The two editions of the Olympics have since alternated every two years during the four-year cycle.[[5]](#cite_note-6)[[6]](#cite_note-7) Lillehammer subsequently launched another bid, now for the 1994 Games, with some drastic modifications of the project, such as a new indoor speed skating venue and an additional ice hall in Lillehammer. Supplementary government guarantees and funds were secured for the new bid.[[7]](#cite_note-8)

Three other locations put in bids for the 1994 Games: [Anchorage](/wiki/Anchorage,_Alaska "Anchorage, Alaska") (United States), [Östersund](/wiki/%C3%96stersund "Östersund") (Sweden), and [Sofia](/wiki/Sofia "Sofia") (Bulgaria). Lillehammer was elected to host the 1994 Winter Games at the 94th IOC Session, held in [Seoul](/wiki/Seoul "Seoul") on 15 September 1988, two days before the start of the [1988 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1988_Summer_Olympics "1988 Summer Olympics").[[8]](#cite_note-9)

## Organization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1994_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Organization")]

Main article: [Lillehammer Olympic Organizing Committee](/wiki/Lillehammer_Olympic_Organizing_Committee "Lillehammer Olympic Organizing Committee")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Lysg%C3%A5rd.JPG/250px-Lysg%C3%A5rd.JPG)](/wiki/File:Lysg%C3%A5rd.JPG)

The ski jumping hill [Lysgårdsbakken](/wiki/Lysg%C3%A5rdsbakken "Lysgårdsbakken") was the venue of the [opening](/wiki/1994_Winter_Olympics_opening_ceremony "1994 Winter Olympics opening ceremony") and [closing](/wiki/1994_Winter_Olympics_closing_ceremony "1994 Winter Olympics closing ceremony") ceremonies

The overall responsibility for the games was held by the Lillehammer Olympic Organizing Committee, which was created on 14 November 1988 and led by [Gerhard Heiberg](/wiki/Gerhard_Heiberg "Gerhard Heiberg").[[10]](#cite_note-looci19-11) Between 1989 and 1993, the committee was reorganized several times with various subsidiaries. On 11 February 1993, it became a joint venture owned 51% by Lillehammer Municipality (as main stakeholder), 24.5% by the Government of Norway, and 24.5% by the [Norwegian Olympic Committee](/wiki/Norwegian_Olympic_Committee "Norwegian Olympic Committee"). This model was also used for the Lillehammer Paralympic Organizing Committee.[[11]](#cite_note-12) The Norwegian government had issued a guarantee for the Games and also covered the expenses related to infrastructure.[[10]](#cite_note-looci19-11) The total cost of the Games was 7.4 billion [Norwegian krone](/wiki/Norwegian_krone "Norwegian krone") (NOK), of which NOK 0.95 billion was expended by the ministries, NOK 4.48 billion was for operations and event expenses, and NOK 1.67 billion was for investments.[[12]](#cite_note-13) The Games had a revenue of NOK 2.71 billion, of which NOK 1.43 billion was from television rights, NOK 0.65 billion was from sponsors, and NOK 0.15 billion was from ticket sales.[[13]](#cite_note-14)

Production and execution of the broadcasting, which cost NOK 462 million,[[14]](#cite_note-15) was a joint venture of the [Norwegian Broadcasting Corporation](/wiki/Norwegian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Norwegian Broadcasting Corporation") (NRK), the [CTV (Canada)](/wiki/CTV_Television_Network "CTV Television Network") (CTV), and the [European Broadcasting Union](/wiki/European_Broadcasting_Union "European Broadcasting Union") (EBU) (the NRK is part of the collaboration of EBU members).
[[15]](#cite_note-16) NRK and EBU had 1,424 people working at the Olympics, while international broadcasters sent an additional 4,050 accredited broadcasting personnel. The transmission rights for the games were held by EBU in Europe, [CBS](/wiki/Olympics_on_CBS "Olympics on CBS") in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), [NHK](/wiki/NHK "NHK") in Japan, [CTV](/wiki/CTV_Television_Network "CTV Television Network") in Canada, the [Asia-Pacific Broadcasting Union](/wiki/Asia-Pacific_Broadcasting_Union "Asia-Pacific Broadcasting Union"), [Nine Network](/wiki/Nine_Network "Nine Network") in Australia, as well as other broadcasters in other countries. The total transmission rights price was 350 million United States dollars, 310 million of which were paid only by CBS. This value was attributed in part to the Harding–Kerrigan affair.[[16]](#cite_note-17) As of 2010, the viewership in the United States was still the highest ever for the Winter Olympics.[[17]](#cite_note-tv-18)

NOK 460 million was used on information technology,[[18]](#cite_note-loocii5-19) with the main system running on an [IBM AS/400](/wiki/IBM_AS/400 "IBM AS/400").[[19]](#cite_note-20) 3,500 media terminals were in use during the games based on the Info '94 system; it was the first Winter Olympics to have terminals installed abroad.[[18]](#cite_note-loocii5-19) [Seiko](/wiki/Seiko "Seiko") delivered the time-keeping devices.[[20]](#cite_note-21) Telecommunications were delivered by [Telenor](/wiki/Telenor "Telenor"), including signal transmission.[[21]](#cite_note-22) This included a mobile radio network with nine base stations.[[22]](#cite_note-23)

## Cost and cost overrun

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1994_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Cost and cost overrun")]

*The Oxford Olympics Study* established the outturn cost of the Lillehammer 1994 Winter Olympics at US$2.2 billion in 2015-dollars and cost overrun at 277% in real terms.[[23]](#cite_note-24) This includes sports-related costs only, that is, (i) *operational costs* incurred by the organizing committee for the purpose of staging the Games, e.g., expenditures for technology, transportation, workforce, administration, security, catering, ceremonies, and medical services, and (ii) *direct capital costs* incurred by the host city and country or private investors to build, e.g., the competition venues, the Olympic village, international broadcast center, and media and press center, which are required to host the Games. Indirect capital costs are *not* included, such as for road, rail, or airport infrastructure, or for hotel upgrades or other business investment incurred in preparation for the Games but not directly related to staging the Games. The cost and cost overrun for Lillehammer 1994 compares with costs of US$2.5 billion and a cost overrun of 13% for Vancouver 2010, and costs of US$51[[24]](#cite_note-The_Guardian-25) billion and a cost overrun of 289% for Sochi 2014, the latter being the most costly Olympics to date. Average cost for Winter Games since 1960 is US$3.1 billion, average cost overrun is 142%.

### Opening ceremony

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1994_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Opening ceremony")]

Main article: [1994 Winter Olympics opening ceremony](/wiki/1994_Winter_Olympics_opening_ceremony "1994 Winter Olympics opening ceremony")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Hammar_Ol%C3%ADmpic.jpg/250px-Hammar_Ol%C3%ADmpic.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hammar_Ol%C3%ADmpic.jpg)

Mexican artist [Abel Ramírez Águilar](/wiki/Abel_Ram%C3%ADrez_%C3%81guilar "Abel Ramírez Águilar") with an ice sculpture he created before the start of the Lillehammer Games

As the 1988 Winter Games, the Organizing Committee decided not to build a specific Olympic Stadium for the opening and closing ceremonies and opted to conduct them at the ski jumping hill, [Lysgårdsbakken](/wiki/Lysg%C3%A5rdsbakken "Lysgårdsbakken"). Artistic content presented a range of Norwegian and Nordic cultures, including elements such as [Sami](/wiki/Sami_people "Sami people") [joik](/wiki/Joik "Joik"), [Telemark skiing](/wiki/Telemark_skiing "Telemark skiing"), fiddlers and folk dancing,[[25]](#cite_note-26) simulations of Norwegian traditional events, such as wedding processions, and the figure of [vetter](/wiki/V%C3%A6ttir "Vættir") from [Norse mythology](/wiki/Norse_mythology "Norse mythology").[[26]](#cite_note-27) After speeches by Heiberg and IOC president [Juan Antonio Samaranch](/wiki/Juan_Antonio_Samaranch "Juan Antonio Samaranch"), the Games were officially declared opened by [King Harald V](/wiki/Harald_V_of_Norway "Harald V of Norway").[[27]](#cite_note-28) and the [Olympic Anthem](/wiki/Olympic_Anthem "Olympic Anthem") was performed by the lyrical singer, [Sissel Kyrkjebø](/wiki/Sissel_Kyrkjeb%C3%B8 "Sissel Kyrkjebø"). After, the [Olympic Flame](/wiki/Olympic_Flame "Olympic Flame") was to be carried down the ski jump before lighting the cauldron. Originally this task had rested upon [Ole Gunnar Fidjestøl](/wiki/Ole_Gunnar_Fidjest%C3%B8l "Ole Gunnar Fidjestøl"), but after he broke an arm in a practice jump, his back-up, [Stein Gruben](/w/index.php?title=Stein_Gruben&action=edit&redlink=1 "Stein Gruben (page does not exist)"), received the honor. The cauldron was lit by [Crown Prince Haakon Magnus](/wiki/Crown_Prince_Haakon_Magnus "Crown Prince Haakon Magnus"). The [Olympic oaths](/wiki/Olympic_oath "Olympic oath") were taken by [Vegard Ulvang](/wiki/Vegard_Ulvang "Vegard Ulvang") for the athletes and [Kari Kåring](/wiki/Kari_K%C3%A5ring "Kari Kåring") for the officials.[[28]](#cite_note-29)

### Alpine skiing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1994_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Alpine skiing")]

Main article: [Alpine skiing at the 1994 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Alpine_skiing_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Alpine skiing at the 1994 Winter Olympics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Stamp_of_Azerbaijan_302.jpg/250px-Stamp_of_Azerbaijan_302.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stamp_of_Azerbaijan_302.jpg)

Since the 1992 Games, the rules for combined changed, where the winner was determined by total time instead of points. The [women's downhill](/wiki/Alpine_skiing_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_downhill "Alpine skiing at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Women's downhill") was originally scheduled for [Hafjell](/wiki/Hafjell "Hafjell"), but after protests due the hill difficulties it was moved to [Kvitfjell](/wiki/Kvitfjell "Kvitfjell"), which also hosted the [men's downhill](/wiki/Alpine_skiing_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_downhill "Alpine skiing at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Men's downhill") and [super-G](/wiki/Alpine_skiing_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_super-G "Alpine skiing at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Men's super-G"). In the men's events, Germany's [Markus Wasmeier](/wiki/Markus_Wasmeier "Markus Wasmeier") won two disciplines, [giant slalom](/wiki/Alpine_skiing_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_giant_slalom "Alpine skiing at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Men's giant slalom") and super-G, finishing ahead of the United States's [Tommy Moe](/wiki/Tommy_Moe "Tommy Moe") on the super-G. Moe won the downhill ahead of Norway's [Kjetil André Aamodt](/wiki/Kjetil_Andr%C3%A9_Aamodt "Kjetil André Aamodt"), who came in third in the super-G. Austria's [Thomas Stangassinger](/wiki/Thomas_Stangassinger "Thomas Stangassinger") won the [slalom](/wiki/Alpine_skiing_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_slalom "Alpine skiing at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Men's slalom") ahead of Italy's [Alberto Tomba](/wiki/Alberto_Tomba "Alberto Tomba"). In the [combined](/wiki/Alpine_skiing_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_combined "Alpine skiing at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Men's combined"), Norway took a medal sweep, with [Lasse Kjus](/wiki/Lasse_Kjus "Lasse Kjus") winning ahead of Aamodt and [Harald Christian Strand Nilsen](/wiki/Harald_Christian_Strand_Nilsen "Harald Christian Strand Nilsen").[[29]](#cite_note-ask-30)

In the women's events, Switzerland's [Vreni Schneider](/wiki/Vreni_Schneider "Vreni Schneider") was the most successful, winning the [slalom](/wiki/Alpine_skiing_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_slalom "Alpine skiing at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Women's slalom"), taking silver in [combined](/wiki/Alpine_skiing_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_combined "Alpine skiing at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Women's combined") and taking bronze in [giant slalom](/wiki/Alpine_skiing_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_giant_slalom "Alpine skiing at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Women's giant slalom"). The only other athlete to take multiple medals was Italy's [Isolde Kostner](/wiki/Isolde_Kostner "Isolde Kostner"), who took a third place in both downhill and [super-G](/wiki/Alpine_skiing_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_super-G "Alpine skiing at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Women's super-G"). The downhill was won by Germany's [Katja Seizinger](/wiki/Katja_Seizinger "Katja Seizinger"), super-G by the United States' [Diann Roffe](/wiki/Diann_Roffe "Diann Roffe"), the giant slalom by Italy's [Deborah Compagnoni](/wiki/Deborah_Compagnoni "Deborah Compagnoni"), and the combined by Sweden's [Pernilla Wiberg](/wiki/Pernilla_Wiberg "Pernilla Wiberg").[[29]](#cite_note-ask-30)

### Biathlon

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1994_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Biathlon")]

Main article: [Biathlon at the 1994 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Biathlon_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Biathlon at the 1994 Winter Olympics")

Russia and Germany split all the individual men's medals. In the [10 km sprint](/wiki/Biathlon_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_sprint "Biathlon at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Men's sprint"), Russia's [Sergei Tchepikov](/wiki/Sergei_Tchepikov "Sergei Tchepikov") won ahead of [Ricco Groß](/wiki/Ricco_Gro%C3%9F "Ricco Groß"), both with a clean sheet.[[30]](#cite_note-31) Bronze winner [Sergei Tarasov](/wiki/Sergei_Tarasov_(biathlete) "Sergei Tarasov (biathlete)") won the [20 km individual](/wiki/Biathlon_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_individual "Biathlon at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Men's individual") ahead of Germany's [Frank Luck](/wiki/Frank_Luck "Frank Luck") and [Sven Fischer](/wiki/Sven_Fischer "Sven Fischer").[[31]](#cite_note-bia-32) Germany easily revenged itself by winning the [4 × 7.5 km relay](/wiki/Biathlon_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_relay "Biathlon at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Men's relay") ahead of Russia and France.[[32]](#cite_note-33) In the women's class, Canada's [Myriam Bédard](/wiki/Myriam_B%C3%A9dard "Myriam Bédard") won both the individual events, finishing ahead of Belarus' [Svetlana Paramygina](/wiki/Svetlana_Paramygina "Svetlana Paramygina") on the [7.5 km sprint](/wiki/Biathlon_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_sprint "Biathlon at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Women's sprint") and ahead of France's [Anne Briand](/wiki/Anne_Briand "Anne Briand") on the [15 km individual](/wiki/Biathlon_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_individual "Biathlon at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Women's individual").[[31]](#cite_note-bia-32) In the [4 × 7.5 km relay](/wiki/Biathlon_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_relay "Biathlon at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Women's relay"), the format since 1992 was changed from three to four participants. Russia, with a clean sheet, won ahead of Germany, who made six misses, with France taking the bronze.[[33]](#cite_note-34)

### Bobsleigh

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1994_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Bobsleigh")]

Main article: [Bobsleigh at the 1994 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Bobsleigh_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Bobsleigh at the 1994 Winter Olympics")

In [two-man](/wiki/Bobsleigh_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Two-man "Bobsleigh at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Two-man"), Switzerland took the top two places, with [Gustav Weder](/wiki/Gustav_Weder "Gustav Weder"), [Donat Acklin](/wiki/Donat_Acklin "Donat Acklin") winning 0.05 seconds ahead of [Reto Götschi](/wiki/Reto_G%C3%B6tschi "Reto Götschi") and [Guido Acklin](/wiki/Guido_Acklin "Guido Acklin"), who were again 0.15 seconds ahead of Italy's [Günther Huber](/wiki/G%C3%BCnther_Huber "Günther Huber") and [Stefano Ticci](/wiki/Stefano_Ticci "Stefano Ticci") placing third.[[34]](#cite_note-35) In [four-man](/wiki/Bobsleigh_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Four-man "Bobsleigh at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Four-man"), Germany-II, consisting of [Harald Czudaj](/wiki/Harald_Czudaj "Harald Czudaj"), [Karsten Brannasch](/wiki/Karsten_Brannasch "Karsten Brannasch"), [Olaf Hampel](/wiki/Olaf_Hampel "Olaf Hampel") and [Alexander Szelig](/wiki/Alexander_Szelig "Alexander Szelig"), finished 0.06 seconds ahead of Switzerland-I and 0.23 ahead of Germany-I.[[35]](#cite_note-36)

### Cross-country skiing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1994_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Cross-country skiing")]

Main article: [Cross-country skiing at the 1994 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Cross-country_skiing_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Cross-country skiing at the 1994 Winter Olympics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Stamp_of_Kazakhstan_039-040.jpg/250px-Stamp_of_Kazakhstan_039-040.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stamp_of_Kazakhstan_039-040.jpg)

Participants from five countries took all the medals of the ten events. Starting in 1994, the Olympics alternated which of the medium-distance and long-distance races had classical and freestyle. [men's 4 × 10 km relay](/wiki/Cross-country_skiing_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_4_%C3%97_10_kilometre_relay "Cross-country skiing at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Men's 4 × 10 kilometre relay") was watched by a crowd of nearly 150,000. Norway, Italy and Finland followed each other tightly for three and a half rounds, with the second and third exchange of the three talking place within 1.1 seconds of each other. Finland fell behind in the end, and Norwegian [Bjørn Dæhlie](/wiki/Bj%C3%B8rn_D%C3%A6hlie "Bjørn Dæhlie") and Italian [Silvio Fauner](/wiki/Silvio_Fauner "Silvio Fauner") battled to the end, with Italy beating Norway by 0.4 seconds.[[36]](#cite_note-37) Dæhlie won the [10 km classical](/wiki/Cross-country_skiing_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_10_kilometre_classical "Cross-country skiing at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Men's 10 kilometre classical") and [15 km freestyle pursuit](/wiki/Cross-country_skiing_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_15_kilometre_freestyle_pursuit "Cross-country skiing at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Men's 15 kilometre freestyle pursuit"), while taking silver in the [30 km freestyle](/wiki/Cross-country_skiing_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_30_kilometre_freestyle "Cross-country skiing at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Men's 30 kilometre freestyle"). Kazakhstan's [Vladimir Smirnov](/wiki/Vladimir_Smirnov_(skier) "Vladimir Smirnov (skier)") won the [50 km classical](/wiki/Cross-country_skiing_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_50_kilometre_classical "Cross-country skiing at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Men's 50 kilometre classical"), in addition to silver in the 10 km and the 15 km. Norway's [Thomas Alsgaard](/wiki/Thomas_Alsgaard "Thomas Alsgaard") won the 30 km, while Finland's [Mika Myllylä](/wiki/Mika_Myllyl%C3%A4 "Mika Myllylä") took an individual silver and a bronze.[[37]](#cite_note-ccs-38)

Italy's [Manuela Di Centa](/wiki/Manuela_Di_Centa "Manuela Di Centa") and Russia's [Lyubov Yegorova](/wiki/Lyubov_Yegorova_(cross-country_skier) "Lyubov Yegorova (cross-country skier)") dominated the women's events. They took five and four medals each, respectively, and between them winning all the races. Yegorova finished ahead of Di Centa on the [5 km classical](/wiki/Cross-country_skiing_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_5_kilometre_classical "Cross-country skiing at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Women's 5 kilometre classical") and the [10 km pursuit](/wiki/Cross-country_skiing_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_10_kilometre_pursuit "Cross-country skiing at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Women's 10 kilometre pursuit"), while Di Centa finished ahead of Yegorova on the [15 km freestyle](/wiki/Cross-country_skiing_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_15_kilometre_freestyle "Cross-country skiing at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Women's 15 kilometre freestyle"), and also won the [30 km classical](/wiki/Cross-country_skiing_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_30_kilometre_classical "Cross-country skiing at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Women's 30 kilometre classical") ahead of Norway's [Marit Wold](/wiki/Marit_Wold "Marit Wold"). Finland's [Marja-Liisa Kirvesniemi](/wiki/Marja-Liisa_Kirvesniemi "Marja-Liisa Kirvesniemi") took two bronze medals, in 5 km and 30 km.[[37]](#cite_note-ccs-38) In the [4 × 5 km relay](/wiki/Cross-country_skiing_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_4_%C3%97_5_kilometre_relay "Cross-country skiing at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Women's 4 × 5 kilometre relay"), Norway and Russia kept up with each other until the final stage, in which [Anita Moen](/wiki/Anita_Moen "Anita Moen") lost to Yegorova, with Italy finishing third. With Yegorova's sixth career gold, she was tied as the most-winning Winter Olympic participant.[[38]](#cite_note-39)

### Figure skating

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1994_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Figure skating")]

Main article: [Figure skating at the 1994 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Figure skating at the 1994 Winter Olympics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Stamp_of_Ukraine._Baiul.jpg/250px-Stamp_of_Ukraine._Baiul.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stamp_of_Ukraine._Baiul.jpg)

Oksana Baiul

On 6 January, [Tonya Harding](/wiki/Tonya_Harding "Tonya Harding")'s ex-husband, Jeff Gillooly and his friend Shawn Eckardt, conspired with Shane Stant to [club fellow female figure skater Nancy Kerrigan in the knee](/wiki/Assault_of_Nancy_Kerrigan "Assault of Nancy Kerrigan").[[39]](#cite_note-40) Both Harding and Kerrigan were selected for the Olympic team. After Harding admitted to helping to cover up the attack, the [United States Olympic Committee](/wiki/United_States_Olympic_Committee "United States Olympic Committee") initiated proceedings to remove her from the Olympic team, but Harding retained her place after threatening legal action.[[40]](#cite_note-41) In the [ladies' singles](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Ladies%27_singles "Figure skating at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Ladies' singles"), Ukraine's [Oksana Baiul](/wiki/Oksana_Baiul "Oksana Baiul") narrowly won ahead of Kerrigan and [Chen Lu](/wiki/Chen_Lu_(figure_skater) "Chen Lu (figure skater)"), with Harding finishing eighth.[[41]](#cite_note-42)[[2]](#cite_note-nyt940108-3) In the [men's singles](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_singles "Figure skating at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Men's singles"), Russia's [Alexei Urmanov](/wiki/Alexei_Urmanov "Alexei Urmanov") won ahead of Canada's [Elvis Stojko](/wiki/Elvis_Stojko "Elvis Stojko") and France's [Philippe Candeloro](/wiki/Philippe_Candeloro "Philippe Candeloro").[[42]](#cite_note-43) Relaxation of the rules led to several former stars returning, such as [ice dancing](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Ice_dancing "Figure skating at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Ice dancing") 1984 Champions Great Britain's [Jayne Torvill](/wiki/Jayne_Torvill "Jayne Torvill") and [Christopher Dean](/wiki/Christopher_Dean "Christopher Dean"), who took a bronze behind Russians [Oksana Grishuk](/wiki/Oksana_Grishuk "Oksana Grishuk") and [Evgeny Platov](/wiki/Evgeny_Platov "Evgeny Platov"), and [Maya Usova](/wiki/Maya_Usova "Maya Usova") and [Alexander Zhulin](/wiki/Alexander_Zhulin "Alexander Zhulin").[[43]](#cite_note-44) In [pair skating](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Pairs "Figure skating at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Pairs"), the Russians also took a double, with [Ekaterina Gordeeva](/wiki/Ekaterina_Gordeeva "Ekaterina Gordeeva") and [Sergei Grinkov](/wiki/Sergei_Grinkov "Sergei Grinkov") winning ahead of [Natalia Mishkutenok](/wiki/Natalia_Mishkutenok "Natalia Mishkutenok") and [Artur Dmitriev](/wiki/Artur_Dmitriev "Artur Dmitriev").[[44]](#cite_note-45)

### Freestyle skiing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1994_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Freestyle skiing")]

Main article: [Freestyle skiing at the 1994 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Freestyle_skiing_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Freestyle skiing at the 1994 Winter Olympics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Stamps_of_Azerbaijan%2C_1995-300.jpg/250px-Stamps_of_Azerbaijan%2C_1995-300.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stamps_of_Azerbaijan,_1995-300.jpg)

[Aerials](/wiki/Aerial_skiing "Aerial skiing") was added as a discipline, after it had been a [demonstration sport](/wiki/Demonstration_sport "Demonstration sport") at the previous two games. [Ski ballet](/wiki/Ski_ballet "Ski ballet"), which had been a demonstration sport in 1992, was dropped.[[45]](#cite_note-46) Canada dominated the men's events, with [Jean-Luc Brassard](/wiki/Jean-Luc_Brassard "Jean-Luc Brassard") winning the [men's moguls](/wiki/Freestyle_skiing_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_moguls "Freestyle skiing at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Men's moguls") ahead of Russian [Sergey Shupletsov](/wiki/Sergey_Shupletsov "Sergey Shupletsov").[[46]](#cite_note-47) In the [men's aerials](/wiki/Freestyle_skiing_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_aerials "Freestyle skiing at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Men's aerials"), Switzerlands's [Andreas Schönbächler](/wiki/Andreas_Sch%C3%B6nb%C3%A4chler "Andreas Schönbächler") won ahead of Canada's [Philippe LaRoche](/wiki/Philippe_LaRoche "Philippe LaRoche") and [Lloyd Langlois](/wiki/Lloyd_Langlois "Lloyd Langlois"), with Canadians also claiming the fourth and sixth places.[[47]](#cite_note-48) In the women's disciplines, Norway was the only nation to take two medals; [Stine Lise Hattestad](/wiki/Stine_Lise_Hattestad "Stine Lise Hattestad") won the [moguls](/wiki/Freestyle_skiing_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_moguls "Freestyle skiing at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Women's moguls") ahead of the United States' [Liz McIntyre](/wiki/Elizabeth_McIntyre "Elizabeth McIntyre").[[48]](#cite_note-49) In the [aerials](/wiki/Freestyle_skiing_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_aerials "Freestyle skiing at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Women's aerials"), [Lina Cheryazova](/wiki/Lina_Cheryazova "Lina Cheryazova") won, claiming Uzbekistan's only medal,[[49]](#cite_note-medaltable-50) ahead of Sweden's [Marie Lindgren](/wiki/Marie_Lindgren "Marie Lindgren") and Norway's [Hilde Synnøve Lid](/wiki/Hilde_Synn%C3%B8ve_Lid "Hilde Synnøve Lid").[[50]](#cite_note-51)

### Ice hockey

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1994_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Ice hockey")]

Main article: [Ice hockey at the 1994 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 1994 Winter Olympics")

Twelve teams participated in the ice hockey tournament, divided into two groups. Each played as a [single round robin](/wiki/Round-robin_tournament "Round-robin tournament"), with the four best advancing to the single elimination medal tournament.[[51]](#cite_note-52) Group A saw Finland win all five matches, while the host nation lost all theirs. Also Germany, the Czech Republic and Russia advanced from the group, all with three victories. Group B was won by Slovakia ahead of Canada, Sweden and the United States.[[52]](#cite_note-53) The quarter-finals saw the Czech Republic, the United States, Germany and Slovakia eliminated.[[53]](#cite_note-54) In the semi-finals, Canada beat Finland 5–3, while Sweden beat Russia 4–3.[[54]](#cite_note-55) After the final period of the final, the match was a 2–2 tie, resulting in a shoot-out. After six shots, it was tied 2–2 until Sweden's [Peter Forsberg](/wiki/Peter_Forsberg "Peter Forsberg") beat [Corey Hirsch](/wiki/Corey_Hirsch "Corey Hirsch"), making the Swedes win after [Paul Kariya](/wiki/Paul_Kariya "Paul Kariya") missed his shot. This led to [Tomas Jonsson](/wiki/Tomas_Jonsson "Tomas Jonsson"), [Håkan Loob](/wiki/H%C3%A5kan_Loob "Håkan Loob") and [Mats Näslund](/wiki/Mats_N%C3%A4slund "Mats Näslund") becoming the first three members of the [Triple Gold Club](/wiki/Triple_Gold_Club "Triple Gold Club").[[55]](#cite_note-56)

### Luge

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1994_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Luge")]

Main article: [Luge at the 1994 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Luge_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Luge at the 1994 Winter Olympics")

Italy, Germany and Austria collected all the medals in the luge events. Germany's [Georg Hackl](/wiki/Georg_Hackl "Georg Hackl") won the [men's singles](/wiki/Luge_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_singles "Luge at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Men's singles"), making him the first to defend an Olympic title in the event in thirty years. He finished ahead of Austria's [Markus Prock](/wiki/Markus_Prock "Markus Prock") and Italy's [Armin Zöggeler](/wiki/Armin_Z%C3%B6ggeler "Armin Zöggeler"). In the [doubles](/wiki/Luge_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Doubles "Luge at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Doubles"), the two Italian teams finished on top, with [Kurt Brugger](/wiki/Kurt_Brugger "Kurt Brugger") and [Wilfried Huber](/wiki/Wilfried_Huber "Wilfried Huber") winning ahead of [Hansjörg Raffl](/wiki/Hansj%C3%B6rg_Raffl "Hansjörg Raffl") and [Norbert Huber](/wiki/Norbert_Huber "Norbert Huber"). In the [women's singles](/wiki/Luge_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_singles "Luge at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Women's singles"), Italy's [Gerda Weissensteiner](/wiki/Gerda_Weissensteiner "Gerda Weissensteiner") won ahead of Germany's [Susi Erdmann](/wiki/Susi_Erdmann "Susi Erdmann") and Austria's [Andrea Tagwerker](/wiki/Andrea_Tagwerker "Andrea Tagwerker").[[56]](#cite_note-57) The own debuts was start Nedžad Lomigora from Bosnia and Herzegovina, Marco Feder from Liechtenstein, Sminon J. Payne from Bermuda, Paul Hix from United Kingdom, Josef Svarek from Slovakia, Roger White from Australia.

### Nordic combined

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1994_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Nordic combined")]

Main article: [Nordic combined at the 1994 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Nordic_combined_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Nordic combined at the 1994 Winter Olympics")

Although the events were the same, since the 1992 Games there was a rule change so that instead of jumping three times and taking the points for the best two, the competitors only jumped twice. In the [individual normal hill/15 km](/wiki/Nordic_combined_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Individual "Nordic combined at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Individual"), Japan's [Kenji Ogiwara](/wiki/Kenji_Ogiwara "Kenji Ogiwara") had only lost a single event in the season's [World Cup](/wiki/FIS_Nordic_Combined_World_Cup "FIS Nordic Combined World Cup"), but came in sixth on the hill, which was won by Norway's [Fred Børre Lundberg](/wiki/Fred_B%C3%B8rre_Lundberg "Fred Børre Lundberg"). He won the event after finishing eight-best in the skiing, ahead of Japan's [Takanori Kono](/wiki/Takanori_Kono "Takanori Kono"), Norway's [Bjarte Engen Vik](/wiki/Bjarte_Engen_Vik "Bjarte Engen Vik") and Ogiwara in fourth.[[57]](#cite_note-58) In the [team normal hill/3 x 10 km](/wiki/Nordic_combined_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Team "Nordic combined at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Team"), Japan finished first, third and fifth among the jumpers, giving them a 5:07 minute lead over Norway and finishing 4:49 minutes ahead. Switzerland took the bronze.[[58]](#cite_note-59)

### Short track speed skating

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1994_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Short track speed skating")]

Main article: [Short track speed skating at the 1994 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Short_track_speed_skating_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Short track speed skating at the 1994 Winter Olympics")

Short track speed skating was dominated by [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "South Korea at the 1994 Winter Olympics"), who won four of the six events. After the discipline's debut in 1992, 1994 featured two new events, the [men's 500 meters](/wiki/Short_track_speed_skating_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_500_metres "Short track speed skating at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Men's 500 metres") and the [women's 1000 meters](/wiki/Short_track_speed_skating_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_1000_metres "Short track speed skating at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Women's 1000 metres").[[59]](#cite_note-stsk-60) South Korea's [Chae Ji-Hoon](/wiki/Chae_Ji-Hoon "Chae Ji-Hoon") won the [men's 500 meters](/wiki/Short_track_speed_skating_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_500_metres "Short track speed skating at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Men's 500 metres"), while taking silver on the [1000 meters](/wiki/Short_track_speed_skating_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_1000_metres "Short track speed skating at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Men's 1000 metres") behind countryman [Kim Ki-Hoon](/wiki/Kim_Ki-Hoon "Kim Ki-Hoon"), who defended his 1992 gold. The bronze was won by Canada's [Marc Gagnon](/wiki/Marc_Gagnon "Marc Gagnon"), who won the B final. In the A final, countryman [Derrick Campbell](/wiki/Derrick_Campbell "Derrick Campbell") was obstructed by Great Britain's [Nicky Gooch](/wiki/Nicky_Gooch "Nicky Gooch"), who was disqualified. Campbell got up and started celebrating his bronze medal, when he discovered he had not completed the race.[[60]](#cite_note-61)

In the [men's 5000 meter relay](/wiki/Short_track_speed_skating_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_5000_metre_relay "Short track speed skating at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Men's 5000 metre relay"), South Korea did not enter after a fall in the sole qualifying event, which took place in March 1993. Canada fell during the final, which saw Italy take a clear victory ahead of the United States, who were marginally ahead of [Australia](/wiki/Australia_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Australia at the 1994 Winter Olympics"). The United States' [Eric Flaim](/wiki/Eric_Flaim "Eric Flaim") became the first to take Olympic medals in both short track and long track speed skating, while Australia took its first Winter Olympic medal ever.[[61]](#cite_note-62) Six people took the individual medals in the women's events, with the United States' [Cathy Turner](/wiki/Cathy_Turner "Cathy Turner") defending her 1992 gold on the [500 meters](/wiki/Short_track_speed_skating_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_500_metres "Short track speed skating at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Women's 500 metres")[[62]](#cite_note-63) and South Korea's [Chun Lee-Kyung](/wiki/Chun_Lee-Kyung "Chun Lee-Kyung") taking the gold in 1000 meters.[[59]](#cite_note-stsk-60) South Korea won the [3000 meter relay](/wiki/Short_track_speed_skating_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_3000_metre_relay "Short track speed skating at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Women's 3000 metre relay") with a team of four girls under 19. At 13, [Kim Yoon-Mi](/wiki/Kim_Yun-Mi_(speed_skater) "Kim Yun-Mi (speed skater)") became the world's youngest Olympic gold medalist.[[63]](#cite_note-64)

### Ski jumping

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1994_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Ski jumping")]

Main article: [Ski jumping at the 1994 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Ski_jumping_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Ski jumping at the 1994 Winter Olympics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Stamps_of_Azerbaijan%2C_1995-299.jpg/250px-Stamps_of_Azerbaijan%2C_1995-299.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stamps_of_Azerbaijan,_1995-299.jpg)

Norway won three of the six individual medals, with Norway's [Espen Bredesen](/wiki/Espen_Bredesen "Espen Bredesen") winning the [normal hill](/wiki/Ski_jumping_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Normal_hill_individual "Ski jumping at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Normal hill individual") ahead of Norway's [Lasse Ottesen](/wiki/Lasse_Ottesen "Lasse Ottesen") and Germany's [Dieter Thoma](/wiki/Dieter_Thoma "Dieter Thoma"). In the [large hill](/wiki/Ski_jumping_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Large_hill_individual "Ski jumping at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Large hill individual"), Germany's [Jens Weißflog](/wiki/Jens_Wei%C3%9Fflog "Jens Weißflog") won ahead of Bredesen and Austria's [Andreas Goldberger](/wiki/Andreas_Goldberger "Andreas Goldberger").[[64]](#cite_note-65) In the
[large hill team](/wiki/Ski_jumping_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Large_hill_team "Ski jumping at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Large hill team"), the 1994 Games introduced new rules whereby all four jumps in each round counted, and not just the best three. Neither Norway nor Finland, who between them had won all but one former Olympic team jump, managed to collect a medal. The event became a duel between Germany and Japan, with only a point separating them after the first round of jumps. [Masahiko Harada](/wiki/Masahiko_Harada "Masahiko Harada") had the last jump, and would secure a gold if he managed 105 meters but lost his 'cool' mistiming his leap and landed at 97.5 meters, giving the gold to the Germans.[[65]](#cite_note-66)

### Speed skating

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1994_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Speed skating")]

Main article: [Speed skating at the 1994 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Speed_skating_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Speed skating at the 1994 Winter Olympics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Stamps_of_Azerbaijan%2C_1995-298.jpg/250px-Stamps_of_Azerbaijan%2C_1995-298.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stamps_of_Azerbaijan,_1995-298.jpg)

In 1989, the International Skating Union mandated that, from the 1994 Olympics onwards, speed skating events would be held indoors to avoid interference from wind and weather conditions in the competitions. The 1994 Games also introduced a new system of qualification rules, limiting the number of participants in the [men's 5000 meters](/wiki/Speed_skating_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_5000_metres "Speed skating at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Men's 5000 metres") and [women's 3000 meters](/wiki/Speed_skating_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_3000_metres "Speed skating at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Women's 3000 metres") to 32, and only allowing the 16 best in each of these events to participate in the [men's 10000 meters](/wiki/Speed_skating_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_10000_metres "Speed skating at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Men's 10000 metres") and the [women's 5000 meters](/wiki/Speed_skating_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_5000_metres "Speed skating at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Women's 5000 metres").

Norway's [Johann Olav Koss](/wiki/Johann_Olav_Koss "Johann Olav Koss") took three golds, in the [men's 1,500 meters](/wiki/Speed_skating_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_1500_metres "Speed skating at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Men's 1500 metres"), 5000 meters and 10000 meters. In the latter two, he finished ahead of fellow countryman [Kjell Storelid](/wiki/Kjell_Storelid "Kjell Storelid"). The [men's 500 meters](/wiki/Speed_skating_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_500_metres "Speed skating at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Men's 500 metres") was won by Russia's [Aleksandr Golubev](/wiki/Aleksandr_Golubev_(speed_skater) "Aleksandr Golubev (speed skater)") ahead of fellow countryman [Sergey Klevchenya](/wiki/Sergey_Klevchenya "Sergey Klevchenya"), while the [men's 1000 meters](/wiki/Speed_skating_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_1000_metres "Speed skating at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Men's 1000 metres") was won by American [Dan Jansen](/wiki/Dan_Jansen "Dan Jansen"). For women, American [Bonnie Blair](/wiki/Bonnie_Blair "Bonnie Blair") defended her two 1992 golds in [500 meters](/wiki/Speed_skating_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_500_metres "Speed skating at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Women's 500 metres") and [1000 meters](/wiki/Speed_skating_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_1000_metres "Speed skating at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Women's 1000 metres"). Austria's [Emese Hunyady](/wiki/Emese_Hunyady "Emese Hunyady") won the [1500 meters](/wiki/Speed_skating_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_1500_metres "Speed skating at the 1994 Winter Olympics – Women's 1500 metres") ahead of Russia's [Svetlana Bazhanova](/wiki/Svetlana_Bazhanova "Svetlana Bazhanova") and Germany's [Gunda Niemann](/wiki/Gunda_Niemann "Gunda Niemann"). However, Bazhanova took gold ahead of Nemeth-Hunyady on the 3000 meters, with Germany's [Claudia Pechstein](/wiki/Claudia_Pechstein "Claudia Pechstein") in third. Pechstein would go on to win the 5000 meters ahead of Niemann.[[66]](#cite_note-67)

### Closing ceremony

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1994_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Closing ceremony")]

Main article: [1994 Winter Olympics closing ceremony](/wiki/1994_Winter_Olympics_closing_ceremony "1994 Winter Olympics closing ceremony")

At the closing ceremonies, also held at Lysgårdsbakken, all spectators were handed a flashlight with the inscription "Remember Sarajevo"—the host of the [1984 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1984_Winter_Olympics "1984 Winter Olympics") which was at worst moment of the [Bosnian War](/wiki/Bosnian_War "Bosnian War"), the [Siege of Sarajevo](/wiki/Siege_of_Sarajevo "Siege of Sarajevo"). The first entrants on the stage were [Liv Ullmann](/wiki/Liv_Ullmann "Liv Ullmann") and [Thor Heyerdahl](/wiki/Thor_Heyerdahl "Thor Heyerdahl"),[[67]](#cite_note-68) followed by the athletes' precession. After the Olympic flag had been transferred to [Nagano](/wiki/Nagano,_Nagano "Nagano, Nagano") mayor [Tasuka Tsukada](/w/index.php?title=Tasuka_Tsukada&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tasuka Tsukada (page does not exist)"), speeches were held by Lillehammer mayor [Audun Tron](/wiki/Audun_Tron "Audun Tron"), Heiberg and Samaranch. The latter used his speech to remind about Sarajevo's situation,[[68]](#cite_note-69) before giving Heiberg IOC's gold medal.[[69]](#cite_note-70) Artistic presentations followed with many of the themes from the opening ceremony.At the 15-minute presentation as the next host city, Nagano was presented to world as a modern [Yama-uba](/wiki/Yama-uba "Yama-uba"), also the [1998 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1998_Winter_Olympics "1998 Winter Olympics") mascots, the [Snowlets](/wiki/Sukki,_Nokki,_Lekki_and_Tsukki "Sukki, Nokki, Lekki and Tsukki"), was also presented on a public eye for the first time. Of the 2,200 people performing in the opening and closing ceremonies, only 50 were professionals.[[70]](#cite_note-71)

## Venues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1994_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Venues")]

Main article: [Venues of the 1994 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Venues_of_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Venues of the 1994 Winter Olympics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/1994_Winter_Olympics_venue_map.svg/250px-1994_Winter_Olympics_venue_map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:1994_Winter_Olympics_venue_map.svg)

Map of the venues

The games were spread out over ten venues in five [municipalities](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Norway "Municipalities of Norway") in two [counties](/wiki/Counties_of_Norway "Counties of Norway"), [Oppland](/wiki/Oppland "Oppland") and [Hedmark](/wiki/Hedmark "Hedmark"). [Lillehammer](/wiki/Lillehammer "Lillehammer"), with 25,000 inhabitants, and [Hamar](/wiki/Hamar "Hamar") and [Gjøvik](/wiki/Gj%C3%B8vik_(town) "Gjøvik (town)"), both with 27,000 inhabitants, are all situated on the lake [Mjøsa](/wiki/Mj%C3%B8sa "Mjøsa"). Gjøvik and Hamar are located 45 and 54 kilometers (28 and 34 mi) south of Lillehammer. Hunderfossen is located 15 kilometers (9.3 mi) north of Lillehammer, but lies within the municipality. Øyer and Ringebu, both with just under 5,000 inhabitants, are located 18 and 50 kilometers (11 and 31 mi) north of Lillehammer, in the valley [Gudbrandsdalen](/wiki/Gudbrandsdalen "Gudbrandsdalen"). Lillehammer had four venues, Hamar had two venues, while Hunderfossen, Gjøvik, Øyer and Ringebu had one venue each.[[71]](#cite_note-72)

In Lillehammer, [Lysgårdsbakken](/wiki/Lysg%C3%A5rdsbakken "Lysgårdsbakken") features twin [ski jumping hills](/wiki/Ski_jumping_hill "Ski jumping hill"). The large hill has a hill size of 138 and a critical point of 120, while the normal hill has a hill size of 100 and a critical point of 90. The hill has capacity for 35,000 spectators and hosted, in addition to the ski jumping events, the opening and closing ceremonies.[[72]](#cite_note-73) [Birkebeineren Skistadion](/wiki/Birkebeineren_Skistadion "Birkebeineren Skistadion") featured cross-country skiing and biathlon, with the stadium itself having a capacity for 31,000 spectators during cross-country skiing and 13,500 during biathlon. In addition, spectators could watch from along the tracks.[[73]](#cite_note-74) [Kanthugen Freestyle Arena](/wiki/Kanthugen_Freestyle_Arena "Kanthugen Freestyle Arena") featured a capacity for 15,000 spectators.[[74]](#cite_note-75) All the outdoor skiing arenas had free areas, which saw up to 25,000 extra spectators at the team jump and 75,000 extra spectators at the 50 km.[[75]](#cite_note-76)

[Lillehammer Olympic Bobsleigh and Luge Track](/wiki/Lillehammer_Olympic_Bobsleigh_and_Luge_Track "Lillehammer Olympic Bobsleigh and Luge Track") is located at [Hunderfossen](/wiki/Hunderfossen "Hunderfossen"). It had a capacity for 10,000 spectators and is the only bobsleigh and luge track in the [Nordic countries](/wiki/Nordic_countries "Nordic countries").[[76]](#cite_note-77) Ice hockey was played at two venues, in [Håkons Hall](/wiki/H%C3%A5kons_Hall "Håkons Hall") in Lillehammer and [Gjøvik Olympic Cavern Hall](/wiki/Fjellhallen "Fjellhallen") in Gjøvik. Håkons Hall has a capacity for 10,500 spectators, and also features the [Norwegian Olympic Museum](/wiki/Norwegian_Olympic_Museum "Norwegian Olympic Museum"). The Cavern Hall is built as a man-made cave and had a capacity for 5,300 spectators.[[77]](#cite_note-78)[[78]](#cite_note-79) Skating events took place at two venues in Hamar. [Vikingskipet](/wiki/Vikingskipet "Vikingskipet") had a capacity for 10,600 spectators and featured speed skating events,[[79]](#cite_note-80) while figure skating and short-track speed skating were held at [Hamar Olympic Amphitheatre](/wiki/Hamar_Olympic_Amphitheatre "Hamar Olympic Amphitheatre").[[80]](#cite_note-81) Alpine skiing was split between two ski resorts: [Hafjell](/wiki/Hafjell "Hafjell") in Øyer and [Kvitfjell](/wiki/Kvitfjell "Kvitfjell") in Ringebu. The former was used for the slalom and giant slalom, while the latter hosted downhill and super-G.[[81]](#cite_note-82)

Spectators relied heavily on the use of buses and trains for transportation. Downtown Lillehammer and the axis between Lillehammer and Oslo were the most limiting areas, and the [Norwegian State Railways](/wiki/Norwegian_State_Railways_(1883%E2%80%931996) "Norwegian State Railways (1883–1996)") ran up to 22 trains per day between Oslo and Lillehammer. Trains were also used northwards towards [Trondheim](/wiki/Trondheim "Trondheim"), while other areas were served by bus. All the venues were located along railway lines, making use of spectators walking from the stations to the venues to limit road congestion, although special services were available for disabled people. Shuttle buses were established between venues and also connected to [park and ride](/wiki/Park_and_ride "Park and ride") facilities.[[82]](#cite_note-83)

## Participating National Olympic Committees

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1994_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Participating National Olympic Committees")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Vikingskipet-Hamar.jpg/250px-Vikingskipet-Hamar.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vikingskipet-Hamar.jpg)

[Vikingskipet](/wiki/Vikingskipet "Vikingskipet") in [Hamar](/wiki/Hamar "Hamar") was the venue for [speed skating](/wiki/Speed_skating_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Speed skating at the 1994 Winter Olympics").

A record 67 nations participated in the 1994 Winter Olympics. These Games were the first to implement stricter qualifying standards that prevented low-performing athletes from competing without meeting minimum requirements. As a consequence, eleven "warm-weather countries" that signed up to take part in the Games were mostly absent because very few of their athletes succeeded in qualifying; the number of African athletes fell from nineteen in 1992 to three in 1994. These rules were, however, not applied to bobsled events, enabling the [U.S. Virgin Islands](/wiki/Virgin_Islands_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Virgin Islands at the 1994 Winter Olympics"), [Trinidad and Tobago](/wiki/Trinidad_and_Tobago_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Trinidad and Tobago at the 1994 Winter Olympics") and [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Jamaica at the 1994 Winter Olympics") to compete in that sport.[[83]](#cite_note-84) On 25 October 1993, the [United Nations General Assembly](/wiki/United_Nations_General_Assembly "United Nations General Assembly") urged its members to observe the [Olympic truce](/wiki/Olympic_truce "Olympic truce"), lasting from seven days before the start of the Games until seven days after the close, making the Lillehammer Olympics the first to observe the truce.[[84]](#cite_note-85) The IOC appealed for a truce in the ongoing [Bosnian War](/wiki/Bosnian_War "Bosnian War") and the [Siege of Sarajevo](/wiki/Siege_of_Sarajevo "Siege of Sarajevo"), the city that had hosted the [1984 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1984_Winter_Olympics "1984 Winter Olympics").[[85]](#cite_note-86)

The former Soviet republics of [Armenia](/wiki/Armenia_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Armenia at the 1994 Winter Olympics"), [Belarus](/wiki/Belarus_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Belarus at the 1994 Winter Olympics"), [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Georgia at the 1994 Winter Olympics"), [Kazakhstan](/wiki/Kazakhstan_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Kazakhstan at the 1994 Winter Olympics"), [Kyrgyzstan](/wiki/Kyrgyzstan_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Kyrgyzstan at the 1994 Winter Olympics"), [Moldova](/wiki/Moldova_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Moldova at the 1994 Winter Olympics"), [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Ukraine at the 1994 Winter Olympics") and [Uzbekistan](/wiki/Uzbekistan_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Uzbekistan at the 1994 Winter Olympics") participated as independent nations. This was the first time since the [1912 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1912_Summer_Olympics "1912 Summer Olympics") that [Russia](/wiki/Russia_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Russia at the 1994 Winter Olympics") competed independently at the Olympic Games. Athletes of these countries previously competed in the Winter Olympics as part of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") team until 1991. The [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Czech Republic at the 1994 Winter Olympics") and [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Slovakia at the 1994 Winter Olympics") participated for the first time, after the break-up of [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia_at_the_Olympics "Czechoslovakia at the Olympics") in 1993. [Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Bosnia and Herzegovina at the 1994 Winter Olympics") made their Olympic debut, following their independence from [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Yugoslavia_at_the_Olympics "Yugoslavia at the Olympics") in 1992; the composition of their four-man bob team was one Croat, two Bosniaks and a Serb, mirroring the ethnic diversity of the country. This was also [Israel](/wiki/Israel_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Israel at the 1994 Winter Olympics")'s first appearance at the Winter Olympics and a member of the [European Olympic Committees](/wiki/European_Olympic_Committees "European Olympic Committees"). [American Samoa](/wiki/American_Samoa_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "American Samoa at the 1994 Winter Olympics") participated for the first time, as did Trinidad and Tobago.
